title: Home

title: Start here
snippet:
Pick the track that matches what you want to do. 
   
     
       How the layers stack 
       A guided tour from the disc image down to per-asset rendering. Interactive layer diagram you can click t

title: Where we are
snippet:
Disc + asset preservation 
       Mode2/2352 reader, ISO9660 walker, PROT.DAT TOC math, format detectors covering all documented PROT format families — verified by a disc-gated categorize_coverage tes

title: How the layers stack
snippet: Click any layer to see what crate handles it and what's documented for it.

title: The pipeline at a glance
snippet: Going top-to-bottom: a PSX disc image, then the ISO filesystem, then the project's archive (PROT.DAT), then per-entry containers, then individual sub-assets. Each layer is owned by exactly one crate.

title: The five runtime VMs
snippet: Legaia's runtime is driven by five independent VMs that talk to one shared actor model. Four are clean bytecode dispatchers (actor / move-table / motion / field-event); the fifth (effect) is a per-slo

title: Quick start
snippet: Build the workspace, run the extraction pipeline, browse the assets, and bring up Ghidra in Docker. Follow these in order — each step depends on the one before it.

title: 1 · Build the workspace
snippet:
The repo is a Cargo workspace with edition 2024. One library + binary per crate. 
 git clone https://github.com/AndrewAltimit/legend-of-legaia-re
cd legend-of-legaia-re
cargo build --release
cargo tes

title: 2 · Extract assets from a disc image
snippet:
This repo ships zero Sony bytes. You need a  .bin  Mode2/2352 image of  Legend of Legaia  (the NA SCUS-94254 build). Anything else (PAL, JP, Demo) won't match the embedded SHA-256 fingerprints. 
 ./ta

title: 3 · Browse the extracted assets
snippet:
The  asset-viewer  binary is a winit + wgpu app with five modes.  A WASM build of the viewer also runs in this browser . 
 ./target/release/asset-viewer prot extracted/PROT.DAT
./target/release/asset-

title: 4 · Stand up Ghidra in Docker
snippet: All static analysis runs through the  blacktop/ghidra  container. The compose file mounts the extracted disc as read-only data and the project + scripts directories as read-write so dumps land back on

title: What this viewer does
snippet:
When you load a full disc, the WASM module: 
   
     Walks ISO9660 to find  PROT.DAT 
     Parses the PROT TOC to locate all 1232 entries 
     Classifies each entry through the same detector chain t

title: Privacy + legal
snippet: This page never uploads your disc image anywhere. The WASM module reads bytes via the browser's  FileReader  API directly into WebAssembly memory, parses them locally, and renders to a canvas. No netw

title: Run engine
snippet: Tick the Legaia engine VMs in-browser using the same  LegaiaRuntime  the native engine-shell uses. Provide your disc image ( .bin  Mode2/2352) —  PROT.DAT  and  CDNAME.TXT  are extracted automatically

title: Subsystems
snippet: How Legaia's engine actually works under the hood. Each card is a self-contained page on one subsystem — what it does, where its code lives, and how it connects to the rest. Start with Boot path if you're new to the project.

title: The shape of the engine
snippet: Legaia is a fairly typical late-90s PSX RPG engine, but with a twist: most of the gameplay logic doesn't live in the main executable.  SCUS_942.54  contains the boot path, low-level I/O, the asset dis

Boot path

World map

title: The clean-room port
snippet:
Engine reimplementation 
       The clean-room Rust port. Phase plan, crate layering, architectural principles, and the legal posture (same as ScummVM / OpenRCT2 / OpenMW: bring your own disc).

title: Boot path
snippet: What the game does between disc-spin and title screen. Three jobs, in order: read the PROT.DAT directory into RAM, wire up the asset-type dispatcher, and hand control to the title-screen overlay.

title: How it works
snippet: Almost every PSX game has a built-in archive that bundles the small files together so the disc can stream them efficiently. Legaia's archive is  PROT.DAT , and it has its own table of contents (TOC) —

title: TOC loader
snippet:
Function FUN_8003E4E8 
     Reads First three sectors of  PROT.DAT  (= 6 KB) 
     Lands at 0x801C70F0  in RAM 
     Called from FUN_8003EFE8  and  FUN_8003F08C  at boot 
   

   The on-disc TOC and t

title: Asset-type dispatcher
snippet:
Function FUN_8001F05C 
     Calling convention result = FUN_8001F05C(byte *src_data, u32 type_and_size, int param3, int copy_only) 
     Encoding type_and_size  packs the type byte in the high 8 bits,

title: Game-mode state machine
snippet: The 28-mode state machine table at  0x8007078C  is the top-level "what is the game doing" dispatcher. Each entry is a  (handler_addr, …)  tuple corresponding to a major mode — boot, title, field, batt

title: Top-level extraction pipeline
snippet:
legaia-extract  (the binary in  crates/extract ) drives the offline preservation pipeline: 
   verify → disc → PROT → categorize → streaming-format extract → TIM → PNG 
   See the  extraction tooling

title: Field / event script VM
snippet: The bytecode interpreter behind Legaia's overworld scripting — NPC movement, dialog triggers, cutscene sequencing, story-flag manipulation. Lives in the town/field overlay at FUN_801DE840. The largest function in the corpus: ~17.5 KB / 4099

title: How it works
snippet: If the  actor VM  animates sprites, the field VM  scripts the world . Every NPC has a small bytecode program that says "walk to this corner, wait, walk back, when the player says hi say something". Ev

title: Opcode groups
snippet: The 43 opcodes cluster into themed groups:

title: Shared NOPs — 0x21 / 0x24 / 0x25 / 0x48
snippet: Four distinct opcode bytes share one handler that just advances PC by 1. Likely reserved/historical.

title: Action & control flow — 0x22–0x26
snippet:
Op Mnemonic Effect 
     
       0x22 EXEC_MOVE Schedule move-table playback. Calls  FUN_800204F8  — the move-table consumer that  crates/mdt  targets. 
       0x23 MOVE_TO Teleport ctx to grid positi

title: Flag-manipulation triplets — 0x2B–0x33
snippet:
The cleanest group — three separate 1-bit-flag banks each with set / clear / test+skip: 
   
     Op Mnemonic Bank 
     
       0x2B / 2C / 2D LFLAG SET/CLR/TST Per-script local flags at  ctx[+0x62]

title: Effects, music, scene transitions — 0x34–0x36

     0x35 BGM  — sub-dispatch

title: Actor / sprite VM
snippet: A small fixed-width bytecode VM driving the title screen's animated sprite cluster. The simplest of Legaia's runtime VMs — 13 opcodes, byte-stream operands, no cross-context targeting. Fully ported.

title: How it works
snippet: If you've ever written a sprite-walk script for an old arcade game, this is exactly that. There's an array of "actors" (a sprite is an actor here), each one carries a small amount of per-instance stat

title: Where it lives
snippet:
Driver FUN_801D6628 
     Dispatch table 0x801CED70  (13 entries) 
     Lives in Title-screen overlay (PROT entry  0971 ) 
     Operand format Byte stream (1 byte opcode + zero-or-more operand bytes p

title: Opcodes
snippet:
The 13 opcodes cover the basics every sprite-animation system needs: 
   
     Spawn / despawn actors. 
     Set / clear a per-actor flag bit (mirrors the lower script-VM banks). 
     Position writes

title: Why it's separate from the field VM
snippet: The actor VM is a fixed-width 13-opcode dispatcher tailored to the title screen's sprite-walk loop. The  field VM  ( FUN_801DE840 ) is a 43-opcode variable-length dispatcher with cross-context targeti

snippet:
Common pre-update  (every byte). Drains the per-frame timer at  +0x54  and the rotation accumulator at  +0x22 .

title: Move-table VM
snippet: A bytecode VM dedicated to per-actor animation, motion, and state. Distinct from the field/event VM and the actor / sprite VM: this one lives in SCUS_942.54, runs on each actor's "move buffer", and is invoked every frame from the actor tick

snippet: If the  actor VM  is the title-screen sprite walker and the  field VM  is the cutscene / NPC scripter, the move VM is the layer in between — a tiny per-actor animation engine. Every actor (a party mem

title: Opcode reference (highlights)
snippet: Sizes are in  u16 units . The full per-case reference is in  docs/subsystems/move-vm.md ; this is the highlight reel.

title: World motion / position
snippet:
Op Mnemonic Size Effect 
     
       0x01 WORLD_ADD 4 actor[+0x14] += v1; +0x16 += v2; +0x2A += v2; +0x18 += v3 
       0x03 WORLD_ROTATE_ADD 2 Adds rotated  v1  into world X/Z using sin/cos table in

title: Animation banks & render
snippet:
Op Mnemonic Size Effect 
     
       0x00 ANIM_BANK_SET 4 actor[+0x3C..+0x40] = v1..v3 << 3

       0x05 RENDER_BANK_ADD 4 actor[+0x24..+0

title: Control flow
snippet:
Op Mnemonic Effect 
     
       0x08 HALT actor[+0x10] |= 0x8 . Sets  bVar3 = false  so the dispatcher exits without advancing PC. 
       0x09 WAIT_SET actor[+0x54] = v1 << 3 . Wait timer;  FUN_8002

title: Flag banks & tween setup
snippet:
Op Mnemonic Effect 
     
       0x31 / 0x32 LFLAG_AND / LFLAG_OR 16-bit per-actor local flag bank at  +0x62 . 
       0x33 CLEAR_BIT_40000000 actor[+0x74] &= ~0x40000000 . 
       0x34 TWEEN_SETUP Lo

title: Control flow
snippet:
The interpreter loops: read opcode at  actor[+0x70] , dispatch, write the new PC  actor[+0x70] += param_3 , then either continue or break depending on  bVar3 . Break opcodes: 
   
     0x08  HALT  — c

title: Extension dispatcher (0x2F) — ported sub-ops
snippet:
Op  0x2F  reads  op[1]  as a 16-bit sub-opcode (range  0x00..0x3C ) and dispatches via the JT at  0x801CE868 .  crates/engine-vm/src/move_vm.rs  ports all 61 sub-opcodes; the highlights: 
   
     Slo

title: Connection to other crates
snippet: crates/mdt  — parses the  MDT format . The per-frame data inside an MDT record is exactly the move-VM bytecode this VM consumes. With the move-VM opcode set documented,  crates/mdt  can grow a disasse

title: Decompile quirks worth knowing
snippet:
Operand units are u16 , not bytes.  op[1]  is the 16-bit operand at offset 2 from the opcode word. 
     PC is also in u16 units.   actor[+0x70] × 2  is the byte offset. 
     param_3  is the size in

title: Motion VM (camera / NPC)
snippet: A small per-actor pursue / patrol / face-target VM at FUN_8003774C in SCUS_942.54. Distinct from the actor / sprite VM (sprite spawn / despawn) and the move-table VM (Tactical Arts / battle animation): this one drives NPC pathing, camera fo

title: How it works
snippet:
Each script entry is  1 + N  bytes: 
   +0  u8 op_byte         ; bit 0x7F = opcode, bit 0x80 = "select target"
+1  u8 target_id       ; only present if bit 0x80 set in op_byte
+N  u8 operand[...]    ;

byte retail addr name semantics

       0x38 80037de0 RotateToAngle yaw rotates toward absolute angle; 12-bit fixed-point ma

+0x1010 896 bytes  32 × 2

title: Effect-ID → human effect name mapping
snippet: Effect IDs are anonymous; no string table maps id → "fireball / thunder / heal". To name effects, trace call sites of  FUN_801DFDF8  in damage / battle-action code (in the town/level-up overlays). Eac

title: Battle
snippet: The battle overlay carries the battle scene loader, the per-actor action state machine, and the effect VM cluster. Loaded at RAM 0x801CE818 — the same load slot as the town overlay, so battle and town never coexist in memory.

title: How it works
snippet: Battles are a separate top-level game mode, and Legaia handles them by loading a dedicated  battle overlay  over the same RAM region the town overlay was using. The whole field state goes away; the ba

title: Inventory
snippet: Battle reads inventory through the same page-banked structure the field VM's op  0x3B SET_ITEM_COUNT  writes: 16 entries × 16-bit per page ×  0x414 -byte stride. The page index is the high nibble of t

title: Status effects

title: Renderer
snippet: Legaia uses a custom variant of the PSX TMD format for its 3D meshes, and a custom renderer to draw it. The renderer is FUN_8002735C — 60 GTE ops, with a per-mode descriptor table that encodes how each primitive group is laid out. The engin

title: How it works
snippet: The PSX has dedicated 3D hardware called the GTE (Geometry Transformation Engine), which transforms vertices, and a dedicated 2D framebuffer with its own 1 MB of VRAM. The way games typically draw a 3

       0x12/13 1 0x09 0x07

title: Cutscene (STR mode)
snippet: Pre-rendered cutscene playback combines PSX STR video (MDEC hardware decoder) with multiplexed XA-ADPCM audio from the XA*.XA files on disc. The engine drives it through game modes 26 and 27 (StrInit / StrMode), which map to SceneMode::Cuts

title: Game modes
snippet:
Index Name param next 
     
       26 STR  ( StrInit ) 0x80A — 
       27 STR MODE  ( StrMode ) 0x000 ConfigInit 
     
   
   StrInit  bootstraps the cutscene: opens the STR stream, initialises the

title: STR sector format
snippet:
STR video is carried in 2048-byte Mode 2 Form 1 sectors. Each sector's user-data area starts with a 20-byte header, followed by 2028 bytes of compressed bitstream payload: 
   Offset  Bytes  Field
0x0

title: MDEC decoder
snippet: MdecDecoder::decode_frame(bs)  converts a complete BS v2 bitstream into an RGBA8 pixel buffer. Clean-room port; reference: PSX-SPX §MDEC Decompression.

title: 1. Bitstream header
snippet: 4 bytes before the macroblock data:  u16 n_words  (number of 32-bit words) +  u16 qs  (per-frame quantization scale, 0–63).

title: 2. VLC decoding
snippet:
Each 8×8 block decodes its DC coefficient first, then AC coefficients until an EOB token. 
   
     DC coefficient  — luma and chroma use separate VLC tables (PSX-SPX Tables B.12 / B.13). Delta-coded

title: 3. Dequantize
snippet:
coef[i] = clamp( (coef[i] * qs * Q_MAT[i] + 4) / 8, -2048, 2047 ) 
   Q_MAT  is the standard PSX quantization matrix. The DC position always gets  qs = 2  applied independently.

title: 4. 8×8 IDCT
snippet: Two-pass separable 2D IDCT using a precomputed cosine table  IDCT_C[k][n]  (pre-scaled by 2048). Row IDCT followed by column IDCT; output clamped to  [-128, 127] .

title: 5. Macroblock layout
snippet: Macroblocks are 16×16 pixels. Each macroblock decodes 6 × 8×8 blocks:  Cr, Cb, Y0 (top-left), Y1 (top-right), Y2 (bottom-left), Y3 (bottom-right) .

title: 6. 4:2:0 upsampling + BT.601 colour conversion
snippet:
Each Cb/Cr sample covers a 2×2 luma region. Chroma values are center-biased (nominal ~128, subtracted before the matrix). 
   R = Y + ((91881 * Cr) >> 16)
G = Y - ((22554 * Cb + 46802 * Cr) >> 16)
B =

title: XA audio
snippet: XA-ADPCM audio is carried on Mode 2 Form 2 sectors with  submode & 0x24 == 0x24 . The demuxer splits them by  (file_no, ch_no)  into per-channel streams. Each 128-byte sound group holds 8 sound units

title: Playback loop (play-str)
snippet:
legaia-engine play-str <file>  demonstrates end-to-end decoding: 
   
     Read the raw file in 2048-byte sectors. 
     Feed each sector to  StrFrameAssembler::push_sector() . 
     On complete frame

title: Engine reimplementation
snippet: A clean-room Rust port of Legend of Legaia. End-user model: ship the engine as a binary; users supply their own disc image; the engine extracts assets at first run and plays the game using fresh Rust ports of every runtime subsystem.

title: How it works
snippet: "Clean-room" here means exactly what it means in the ScummVM / OpenRCT2 / OpenMW / OpenLara projects: every line of code in the engine port is fresh Rust, written from  format documentation + decompil

title: Goal & non-goals
snippet:
Goal:  a playable port of Legend of Legaia (NA SCUS-94254) on modern systems via Rust + wgpu, with optional WASM/web target. JP/EU regions land after NA is solid. 
   Non-goals: 
   
     Improving th

title: Architectural principles
snippet: Asset crates stay engine-agnostic.   crates/tim ,  crates/tmd , etc. don't depend on wgpu / SDL3 / cpal. They produce typed in-memory representations; the engine layer turns those into GPU resources /

snippet:
iso          ← (none)
prot         → iso (conceptual)
lzs          ← (none)
asset        → lzs, prot
tmd          ← (none)
tim          ← (none)
xa           ← (none)
vab          → xa  (shares SPU-AD

snippet: The decompiled C dumps under  ghidra/scripts/funcs/  are reference material. Engine code in  crates/engine-vm/  is fresh Rust written  from  the decompile — never paste, always rewrite from the docume

title: Formats
snippet: Byte-level specs for everything on the disc, each with provenance back to a Ghidra-traced function dump. The full reference for each format is on GitHub; the cards below are quick orientation.

title: Disc + container layer
snippet:
What the disc image looks like before any in-game format gets involved. 
   
     
       PSX disc geometry 
       Mode2/2352 sectors, 24-byte ECC overhead, ISO9660 path resolution. The  iso  crate's

title: Compression + dispatch
snippet:
Legaia LZS 
       Custom LZSS decoder reverse-engineered from  FUN_8001A55C . 4096-byte ring buffer, zero-init. "Decompresses without error" is not a validity signal — always magic-check the decoded

title: Per-asset formats
snippet:
PSX TIM 
       Standard PlayStation texture format. 4 / 8 / 15 bpp; CLUTs scatter across PROT entries.  crates/tim  exports to PNG. 
     
     
       Legaia TMD 
       Custom PSX TMD variant — mag

title: Streaming + scene containers
snippet:
DATA_FIELD streaming 
       Chunked container parsed by  crates/asset::parse_streaming . Sister detector  data_field_truncated  catches the shape where the final chunk's declared size walks past EOF

title: Runtime overlay carriers
snippet:
MIPS overlay code 
       Some PROT entries carry MIPS code that loads into the overlay window at  0x801C0000+ . The detector finds them by SP-prologue density. 
     
     
       Overlay pointer-tab

title: Audio path-strings + placeholders
snippet:
Sound-driver path strings 
       3 SCUS consumers: sound init / streaming-asset loader / mode-aware ext dispatcher. 8 extensions: .MAP, .PCH, .spk, .dpk, etc. 
     
     
       Pochi-filler placeho

title: Tooling
snippet: Extraction CLIs, the headless Ghidra workflow, runtime overlay capture, and the mednafen save-state automation toolkit.

title: Asset extraction
snippet:
Each crate ships its own extraction binary; the top-level  legaia-extract  orchestrates them end-to-end. 
 # Full pipeline
./target/release/legaia-extract \
    "/path/to/Legend of Legaia (USA).bin" -

title: Ghidra in Docker
snippet:
Headless Ghidra runs in a single Docker service. Volumes mount the extracted disc as read-only data plus the project DB and scripts directory as read-write. 
 docker compose up -d ghidra

# One-time:

title: The script catalogue
snippet:
All scripts live in  ghidra/scripts/ : 
   
     dump_funcs.py  — dump a list of functions by entry address. 
     find_lui_writers.py  — find LUI+ADDIU pairs that target a specific 32-bit address. Ne

title: Runtime overlay capture
snippet:
Static analysis hits a wall when code lives in RAM overlays at  0x801C0000+ . Capture works from mednafen save states, PCSX-Redux Lua dumps, or Duckstation  .sav  files. All three are supported: 
 # M

title: Captured overlays
snippet:
All major runtime code paths are now covered: 
   
     Town / field / dialog / inventory  — field VM ( FUN_801DE840 ), MES renderer, inventory hub 
     Battle / battle-action  — per-actor SM ( FUN_8

title: Mednafen automation
snippet: A scriptable substitute for mednafen's interactive memory-watchpoint debugger. The toolkit treats each  .mc{0..9}  save state as a frozen RAM snapshot, then uses pairwise diffs and targeted bisection

title: The crate
snippet:
crates/mednafen  ships: 
   
     MDFNSVST parser  — gzip wrapper + targeted-scan section indexer. Resolves  MAIN.MainRAM.data8  as 2 MiB of PSX main RAM and indexes the  GPU  /  SPU  /  CDC  /  MDEC

title: The CLI
snippet:
# Inspect one save's section table
target/release/mednafen-state info "$HOME/.mednafen/mcs/...mc0"

# Slice an overlay window (replaces extract-mednafen-overlay.py)
target/release/mednafen-state extra

title: The scenarios
snippet:
Ten labelled scenarios cover the gameplay states most useful for blocked decompilation work: 
   
     mc0 drake_castle  — steady-state field with full party (move-table base pointer) 
     mc1/mc2/mc

title: Why scripted snapshots?
snippet: Mednafen's TUI debugger has memory breakpoints but no scriptable interface — every interaction is keyboard-driven inside the running window. Save-state diffs answer the same question ( "between this p

title: Findings unlocked by the toolkit
snippet: MOVE-table source pinned  — tracing  _DAT_8007B888  across the 10 saves shows three distinct values (one per scene); the per-scene CDNAME block's slot-1 PROT entry (a  scene_asset_table ) carries desc

title: Reference
snippet: Lookup tables for key functions, the PSX RAM map, and cross-region build differences.

title: Reference pages
snippet:
Key functions 
       A directory of Ghidra-traced anchor functions across the codebase. Boot, asset loader, the runtime VMs, BIOS thunks, libsnd/libspu, GTE primitives, libcd, the battle and effect s

title: Key functions
snippet: A directory of the Ghidra-traced anchor functions across the codebase. The full per-function reference (with descriptions) is in the markdown doc on GitHub. This page is a quick orientation that groups them by subsystem.

title: Boot + asset loader
snippet:
FUN_8003E4E8  — boot-time TOC loader; reads first 3 sectors of PROT.DAT into  0x801C70F0 
         FUN_8003E8A8  — LBA resolver against the in-RAM TOC 
         FUN_8003E6BC  — path-based file opener

title: Game VMs
snippet:
FUN_801D6628  — actor / sprite VM (13 opcodes, JT at  0x801CED70 ) 
         FUN_80023070  — move-table VM (71 opcodes, JT at  0x80010778 ) 
         FUN_801D362C  — move-VM extension (61 sub-opcodes

title: Renderer + GTE
snippet:
FUN_8002735C  — Legaia TMD renderer (60 GTE ops) 
         FUN_80026B4C  — TMD validate + register 
         FUN_800268DC  — TMD pointer fixup 
         0x80010778  — move-VM jump table 
         DAT_

title: Battle
snippet:
FUN_801E295C  — battle action state machine (16 KB) 
         FUN_801D0748  — battle / level-up main tick; sub-states 0x1E/0x32/0x6E/0xFE 
         FUN_801D388C  — battle animation dispatcher; switch

title: Menu overlay
snippet:
FUN_801DD35C  — top-level menu dispatcher (12 KB, 134 outgoing) 
         FUN_801D33D8  — submenu rendering loop 
         FUN_801E1C1C  — shared menu-element renderer 
         FUN_801CF650  — equipm

title: Audio (PsyQ)
snippet:
FUN_8001FA88  — sound init / .dpk loader 
         FUN_8001FC00  — streaming-asset loader (sound\) 
         FUN_8001EBEC  — mode-aware ext dispatcher 
         FUN_800243F0  — BGM lookup (PROT-relati

title: PSX RAM map
snippet: 2 MB of main RAM at 0x80000000-0x801FFFFF. Click any region to see what lives there.

title: Interactive map
snippet:
Order: low addresses on top. Hover for a one-liner, click for full detail. 

   
     BIOS / OS area 
     
       0x80000000 — 0x8000FFFF 
       PSX BIOS workspace, kernel TCB, exception vectors

title: Notes
snippet: The overlay window holds  different code  depending on which game mode is active. Static analysis only sees what's loaded at the time of capture. The repo has separate overlay imports for: title scree
